threats.title: AgentDAO Vacuous Success: Proposals Auto-Succeed When Token TotalSupply is 0 at Snapshot
threats.hypothesis_description: The AgentDAO.state() override returns ProposalState.Succeeded when forVotes == getPastTotalSupply(snapshot). If the proposal is created in the same block as the DAO/token deployment, the snapshot (clock()-1 = previous block) precedes token minting, making getPastTotalSupply return 0. Since forVotes is also 0, the condition 0==0 evaluates true, making ANY Active proposal immediately Succeeded. Combined with the ContributionNft admin's ability to bypass the proposal threshold, this allows arbitrary proposal execution on newly created AgentDAOs.
threats.investigation_rationale: The 0==0 edge case is a classic vacuous truth bug in governance contracts. The code has no guard against zero totalSupply, and the ContributionNft admin can create proposals bypassing threshold checks, making exploitation straightforward.

proof_of_signal.pattern: forVotes == token().getPastTotalSupply(proposalSnapshot(proposalId)) with no zero-check; 0==0 returns Succeeded

preconditions: AgentDAO is newly created with token supply 0 at snapshot block
ContributionNft admin creates proposal in same block or before token minting
votingDelay is 0 (set in initialize)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: When both are 0, any Active proposal becomes Succeeded; execute() is callable immediately
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No check that getPastTotalSupply > 0 or forVotes > 0

analysis_questions: What is the exact block delay between DAO creation and token minting in AgentFactoryV3?
Can the ContributionNft admin reliably create a proposal before token supply exists at the snapshot?
What assets are accessible through executed proposals on a new AgentDAO?
validation_requirements: Deploy AgentDAO and token, create proposal in same block, verify state() returns Succeeded
Verify execute() succeeds with arbitrary targets when forVotes==totalSupply==0
Test with ContributionNft admin bypassing threshold

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker who is ContributionNft admin creates a proposal targeting the DAO's TBA in the same block as agent creation. Snapshot at block-1 shows 0 supply. state() returns Succeeded. execute() drains TBA assets.
prerequisites: ContributionNft admin access or 0 proposal threshold
Proposal created before token supply exists at snapshot block

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete drain of AgentDAO TBA assets for newly created agents
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All token holders of the affected agent

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for 0-totalSupply edge case in state() override

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: None - just needs ContributionNft admin access
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring proposal creation on new DAOs

threats.title: Genesis.onGenesisSuccess Repeatable: Operator Can Overwrite User Token Claims
threats.hypothesis_description: onGenesisSuccess has no terminal state flag after successful execution. The only guard is refundUserCountForFailed==0, which remains true after a success call. The claimableAgentTokens mapping uses assignment (=) not addition (+=), meaning a second call overwrites all previous unclaimed allocations. OPERATION_ROLE can redirect all unclaimed agent tokens to attacker-controlled addresses.
threats.investigation_rationale: The absence of an isSucceeded flag and the use of = instead of += for claimableAgentTokens creates a concrete re-entrancy in the state machine. A backend bug or compromised OPERATION_ROLE key could trigger a second call.

proof_of_signal.pattern: claimableAgentTokens[addr] = amount (assignment, not +=); no isSucceeded flag check

preconditions: OPERATION_ROLE calls onGenesisSuccess successfully once
Some users have not yet called claimAgentToken
OPERATION_ROLE calls onGenesisSuccess again with different distribution

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: No state flag preventing repeated onGenesisSuccess; claimableAgentTokens uses = not +=
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Second call overwrites unclaimed allocations, redirecting tokens
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No isSucceeded boolean or equivalent terminal state guard

analysis_questions: Can onGenesisSuccess be called twice with the same genesis after agentTokenAddress is set?
Does the second call correctly pass all modifier checks?
What happens to partially claimed vs unclaimed tokens?
validation_requirements: Call onGenesisSuccess twice, verify second call overwrites first allocations
Verify first allocation users lose their claimable tokens
Check if any modifier prevents the second call

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: After legitimate distribution, OPERATION_ROLE calls onGenesisSuccess again with different distributeAgentTokenUserAddresses pointing to attacker addresses. Original users' claimableAgentTokens are zeroed out.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Total loss of all unclaimed agent tokens from genesis
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All genesis participants who haven't claimed

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for repeated onGenesisSuccess calls

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Any time after first successful call
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring duplicate onGenesisSuccess events

threats.title: FERC20 burnFrom Does Not Decrement totalSupply, Corrupting Graduation Token Parameters
threats.hypothesis_description: FERC20.burnFrom reduces _balances[user] but never decrements _totalSupply. During graduation in Bonding._openTradingOnUniswap, _token.data.supply (set from original totalSupply) is used to calculate agent token parameters via executeBondingCurveApplication. After burnFrom(pairAddress, tokenBalance), the phantom supply inflates agent token creation parameters. The 1:1 unwrap mapping in unwrapToken then operates on mismatched supply ratios.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a clear code defect violating ERC20 supply conservation. The downstream impact on agent token creation parameters could result in incorrect token economics for every graduated token.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _balances[user] -= amount but _totalSupply NOT decremented in either _burn or burnFrom

preconditions: Any FERC20 token reaches graduation threshold
_openTradingOnUniswap calls burnFrom on pair address
executeBondingCurveApplication uses _token.data.supply which reflects stale totalSupply

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: burnFrom subtracts from balance but not totalSupply
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: After burn: sum(balances) < totalSupply(); graduation uses inflated supply for agent token creation
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: _totalSupply -= amount is completely missing from both burn functions

analysis_questions: How does the inflated supply parameter affect executeBondingCurveApplication?
Does the agent token created have inflated supply compared to actual FERC20 holders?
Does the 1:1 unwrap in unwrapToken distribute more agent tokens than intended?
validation_requirements: Deploy FERC20, burn tokens, verify totalSupply unchanged
Trace graduation flow to verify supply parameter mismatch
Calculate exact economic impact of inflated agent token supply

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Every token graduation creates an agent token with supply parameters based on inflated FERC20 totalSupply. The extra phantom supply results in more agent tokens minted than there are FERC20 holders to claim them, creating surplus tokens.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Incorrect agent token economics for every graduated token; potential surplus tokens extractable
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All FERC20 holders and future agent token holders
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Cannot be fixed for already graduated tokens

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying totalSupply after burns during graduation

technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Cost to buy enough tokens to trigger graduation
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing totalSupply vs sum of balances

threats.title: Bonding.launch() Always Reverts Due to Double nonReentrant Modifier
threats.hypothesis_description: launch() has nonReentrant modifier and internally calls launchFor() which also has nonReentrant. Since the reentrancy guard is already locked by launch(), the nested call to launchFor() will always revert. This forces all token creation through launchFor() which accepts an arbitrary creator parameter, allowing any caller to attribute token creation to any address.
threats.investigation_rationale: Both functions have the nonReentrant modifier. In OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuardUpgradeable, re-entering a nonReentrant function while the guard is locked causes a revert. The downstream impact is that creator attribution is fully spoofable via launchFor().

proof_of_signal.pattern: launch() has nonReentrant and calls launchFor() which also has nonReentrant; nested reentrancy guard always reverts

preconditions: Any user calls launch()

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Double nonReentrant on internal call chain
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: launch() is permanently broken; launchFor() allows spoofing creator address affecting graduation rewards and AgentTax creator fees
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: launch() should call an internal helper without nonReentrant, or launchFor should not have nonReentrant

analysis_questions: Is launch() called anywhere in the protocol?
What are the downstream effects of arbitrary creator attribution via launchFor()?
Does the creator address affect graduation reward distribution?
validation_requirements: Call launch() and verify it always reverts
Call launchFor() with arbitrary creator and trace impact on graduation and tax distribution
Verify creator attribution affects AgentTax.updateCreator initial value

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker calls launchFor() setting creator to their own address for a popular token concept. During graduation, the attacker receives creator rewards via AgentTax creator fee (99% of tax revenue by default).
prerequisites: Sufficient asset tokens to pay fee and initial purchase

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Creator fee revenue (99% of agent tax) stolen from legitimate creator
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Legitimate token creators who expected to receive creator fees
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Creator can be updated via AgentTax.updateCreator by the recorded creator

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying launch() actually works

threats.title: AgentTax.dcaSell Uses Incorrect Slippage Calculation Based on Input Amount
threats.hypothesis_description: dcaSell calculates minOutput as (amountToSwap * (DENOM - slippage)) / DENOM, treating the input taxToken amount as if it equals the expected assetToken output. If taxToken and assetToken have vastly different values, this calculation is meaningless: either minOutput is far too high (causing permanent DoS) or far too low (providing zero slippage protection). The correct calculation should use router.getAmountsOut to determine expected output first.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a mathematical error in the slippage formula. _swapForAsset (called by handleAgentTaxes) correctly uses router.getAmountsOut for its minOutput, but dcaSell bypasses this pattern with the incorrect input-based calculation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: minOutput = ((amountToSwap * (DENOM - slippage)) / DENOM); uses input token amount as basis for output token slippage

preconditions: EXECUTOR_ROLE calls dcaSell
taxToken and assetToken have different exchange rates (i.e., not 1:1)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: If taxToken is worth much less than assetToken, minOutput is too high and all swaps revert (DoS). If worth more, minOutput provides no protection.
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should call router.getAmountsOut first, then apply slippage to the expected output

analysis_questions: What is the actual exchange rate between taxToken and assetToken in production?
Does dcaSell always revert or always provide zero protection?
How much tax revenue is at risk?
validation_requirements: Calculate minOutput for realistic exchange rates and compare to expected output
Test dcaSell with production token pair to verify it either reverts or allows sandwich
Compare dcaSell slippage calculation to handleAgentTaxes's correct approach

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If taxToken trades at 0.001 assetToken, dcaSell sets minOutput to ~0.999*amountToSwap which is 999x the expected output. All dcaSell calls revert permanently, locking accumulated tax revenue. Alternatively, if taxToken is more valuable, minOutput approaches 0 relative to actual output.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Either permanent DoS on tax distribution or complete value extraction via inadequate slippage
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: handleAgentTaxes can still be used as workaround

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test comparing dcaSell minOutput to actual expected output

threats.title: Deliberate Vote Type Enables Double-Voting in AgentDAO and VirtualGenesisDAO
threats.hypothesis_description: In GovernorCountingSimpleUpgradeable._countVote, voting with support=3 (Deliberate) returns early WITHOUT setting hasVoted[account]=true. This allows the same account to call castVote again with a real vote type (For/Against/Abstain), effectively getting two VoteCast events and the ability to strategically delay their actual vote after observing others. While the on-chain vote weight is only counted once, the emitted events mislead off-chain governance systems.
threats.investigation_rationale: The Deliberate type is intended as a signal-only vote, but by not setting hasVoted, it creates a loophole where a voter can participate twice in the voting process. In AgentDAO specifically, this could affect score tracking if the interaction with _castVote override is not properly handled.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (support == uint8(VoteType.Deliberate)) { return; } — returns without setting hasVoted[account] = true

preconditions: Voter has voting weight on an Active proposal
Voter first calls castVote with support=3 (Deliberate)
Voter then calls castVote again with support=0/1/2

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Early return for Deliberate vote without setting hasVoted flag
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Voter can cast a non-binding Deliberate vote, observe other votes, then cast real vote — breaking one-person-one-vote-timing invariant
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Deliberate should either set hasVoted=true or use separate tracking

analysis_questions: Does the double VoteCast event affect off-chain governance dashboards?
In AgentDAO._castVote, is the score correctly handled when Deliberate precedes a real vote?
Does the strategic voting advantage matter on an L2 with no public mempool?
validation_requirements: Cast Deliberate vote, then cast For vote, verify both succeed
Check AgentDAO score increment behavior across the two votes
Verify VoteCast events emitted for both calls

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Voter with large voting power signals Deliberate to appear neutral, observes other votes on-chain (after they're mined), then casts their actual vote strategically. Two VoteCast events confuse governance aggregators.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Strategic governance manipulation; misleading event emissions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All DAO participants relying on accurate vote tracking
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: N/A — governance decisions may already be affected

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring duplicate VoteCast events per account

threats.title: Genesis.participate Per-Transaction Cap Not Per-User: Whales Can Dominate Allocation
threats.hypothesis_description: Genesis.participate checks virtualsAmt <= maxContributionVirtualAmount per transaction but does NOT check the cumulative mapAddrToVirtuals[msg.sender]. A user can call participate() multiple times with amounts up to maxContributionVirtualAmount each, accumulating far more than the intended cap. This defeats the purpose of limiting individual contributions.
threats.investigation_rationale: The variable name 'maxContributionVirtualAmount' strongly implies a per-user cap, but the implementation only enforces a per-transaction limit. The cumulative mapping is incremented with += but never checked against the cap.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(virtualsAmt <= maxContributionVirtualAmount) checks single tx, not cumulative mapAddrToVirtuals[msg.sender] + virtualsAmt

preconditions: Genesis is in Active state
User has sufficient VIRTUAL tokens and allowance

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Per-tx cap instead of per-user cumulative cap
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: mapAddrToVirtuals[msg.sender] += virtualsAmt accumulates without limit check

analysis_questions: Is maxContributionVirtualAmount intended as a per-user or per-transaction limit?
How does off-chain distribution handle users who over-contributed?
Can a whale receive disproportionate agent tokens?
validation_requirements: Call participate() twice with maxContributionVirtualAmount, verify total exceeds cap
Check if off-chain distribution normalizes or if on-chain state determines allocation

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Whale calls participate(1, maxContributionVirtualAmount) 100 times, contributing 100x the intended maximum. In distribution, they receive a disproportionate share of agent tokens.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All other genesis participants receive diluted allocation
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Off-chain distribution could correct, but on-chain state allows over-contribution

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for cumulative contribution exceeding cap

threats.title: ContributionNft.mint Allows Minting for Non-Accepted (Rejected/Active) Proposals
threats.hypothesis_description: ContributionNft.mint only checks that msg.sender == personaDAO.proposalProposer(proposalId). It does NOT verify the proposal has succeeded via isAccepted(). A proposer can mint ContributionNfts for defeated, active, or cancelled proposals, polluting the contribution tree and parent-child relationships used by ServiceNft.
threats.investigation_rationale: The isAccepted() function exists in the contract but is never enforced during minting. This allows rejected contributions to be recorded on-chain, potentially affecting the contribution graph integrity.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Only checks msg.sender == proposalProposer; no call to isAccepted() or state check

preconditions: User creates a proposal in AgentDAO
Proposal is defeated or still active
User calls ContributionNft.mint with the proposal ID

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Missing proposal state validation in mint
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Rejected proposals get ContributionNfts, polluting contribution tree and potentially affecting reward calculations

analysis_questions: Does ServiceNft.mint depend on the ContributionNft existing?
Can fake ContributionNfts affect reward distribution?
Does the parent-child graph used by ServiceNft trust all ContributionNfts?
validation_requirements: Create a proposal, vote it down, mint ContributionNft for it
Trace downstream effects on ServiceNft and reward calculations

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker proposes a contribution, it gets rejected, but they mint a ContributionNft anyway. This pollutes the contribution tree, and if any downstream system trusts ContributionNft existence as proof of acceptance, it can be exploited.
prerequisites: Ability to create proposals on an AgentDAO

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Contribution tree pollution; potential reward gaming if downstream systems trust NFT existence
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All participants in the affected agent's governance
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: NFTs cannot be burned by others

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying mint requires proposal acceptance

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by comparing proposal state with NFT existence

threats.title: AgentDAO._tryAutoExecute Causes Voter DoS When Proposal Target Reverts
threats.hypothesis_description: _tryAutoExecute is called inside _castVote when support==1 (For). If forVotes reaches totalSupply, execute(proposalId) is called inline during the voter's castVote transaction. If any proposal target reverts, the execute call reverts, which reverts the entire castVote transaction. The final For voter can never successfully vote, permanently blocking unanimous auto-execution.
threats.investigation_rationale: The auto-execute happens inside the voting path. A malicious proposal can intentionally target a reverting contract. Since the revert propagates up through castVote, the vote itself is rejected. This prevents anyone from being the 'last For voter'.

proof_of_signal.pattern: execute(proposalId) called inside _castVote; revert in execution propagates to voter's transaction

preconditions: Proposal targets a contract that will revert on execution
All but one token holder have voted For
Last holder tries to vote For, triggering auto-execute

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: execute() called inline during castVote; no try/catch wrapper
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Reverting target causes voter DoS; no voter can achieve unanimous for-vote
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should wrap execute() in try/catch or separate auto-execution from voting

analysis_questions: Can proposals still succeed via normal execution after voting period ends?
What is the gas cost impact of inline execution during voting?
Can this be used to grief all auto-execution on an AgentDAO?
validation_requirements: Create proposal targeting a reverting contract
Have all holders vote For except one
Verify last For vote always reverts

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker creates a proposal targeting a contract that always reverts (e.g., self-destructed or intentionally reverting receive). When voting reaches n-1 For votes, the last voter's For vote triggers _tryAutoExecute which calls execute, which reverts the entire castVote.
prerequisites: Proposal threshold met or admin bypass

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: DoS on unanimous auto-execution feature; proposals stuck in Active until timeout
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All voters on the affected proposal
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Proposal can still execute after voting period if quorum/majority met

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for auto-execute when proposal target reverts

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring failed castVote transactions

threats.title: Airdrop Contract Missing Array Length Validation and Total Reconciliation
threats.hypothesis_description: The Airdrop.airdrop function uses _amounts.length as the loop bound but reads from both _amounts.offset and _recipients.offset in raw calldata. If _recipients.length < _amounts.length, the loop reads past the recipients array into adjacent calldata, sending tokens to garbage addresses derived from other calldata fields. Additionally, sum(amounts) is never compared to _total, allowing excess tokens to be permanently trapped in the contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: Assembly-based functions bypass Solidity's built-in safety checks. The lack of length validation and total reconciliation are classic assembly pitfalls that can lead to fund loss.

proof_of_signal.pattern: let sz := _amounts.length used as loop bound; _recipients not validated; calldataload reads past recipients array

preconditions: Caller provides mismatched array lengths (_recipients shorter than _amounts)
Or caller provides _total > sum(_amounts)

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Mismatched arrays send tokens to garbage addresses; excess _total tokens stuck permanently
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Array length comparison and total reconciliation

analysis_questions: What calldata values get interpreted as addresses when reading past _recipients?
Can the stuck tokens be recovered?
Is there any caller-side validation before calling airdrop?
validation_requirements: Call airdrop with _recipients.length < _amounts.length, verify garbage address transfers
Call airdrop with _total > sum(_amounts), verify excess stuck in contract

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A misconfigured off-chain system calls airdrop with mismatched arrays. Tokens are sent to unintended addresses derived from adjacent calldata, resulting in irreversible fund loss.
prerequisites: Caller makes an error in array construction

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of airdropped tokens to garbage addresses or contract
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Intended airdrop recipients who don't receive tokens
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: None - tokens at garbage addresses are unrecoverable

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test for mismatched array lengths

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable post-hoc by comparing events to intended recipients

threats.title: Bonding.buy() and sell() Missing nonReentrant: State Updates After External Calls
threats.hypothesis_description: Bonding.buy() and sell() lack nonReentrant modifiers. They read pair reserves, execute router calls (which involve token transfers), and then update tokenInfo state afterwards. If the asset token has transfer hooks (ERC777-compatible) or if any intermediate call allows re-entrance to Bonding, the tokenInfo state can be read in a stale state during re-entry. Critically, the graduation check in buy() occurs AFTER the router call but BEFORE tokenInfo is updated.
threats.investigation_rationale: The checks-effects-interactions pattern is violated: external calls via router happen before tokenInfo state updates. While the router itself has nonReentrant, re-entry into Bonding.buy/sell/unwrapToken from a token callback is not prevented.
deployment_assumptions: Asset token must have transfer hooks or callbacks for reentrancy to be exploitable
threats.config_evidence: Asset token is VIRTUAL (standard ERC20 without hooks per Virtual.sol). Unless a different token is configured, reentrancy via token callbacks is unlikely with standard ERC20.

proof_of_signal.pattern: router.buy() external call at line 178; tokenInfo state updates at lines 181-197; no nonReentrant modifier on buy()

preconditions: Asset token or traded token has transfer callback mechanism
Callback allows re-entry into Bonding functions

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: External calls via router before tokenInfo state updates; no nonReentrant
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Re-entry reads stale tokenInfo; graduation threshold check uses stale reserves
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: nonReentrant modifier on buy() and sell()

analysis_questions: What token types can be traded on the bonding curve?
Does the FERC20 token have any callback mechanism?
Can re-entry trigger double graduation or stale state reads?
validation_requirements: Deploy with ERC777-compatible asset token, test reentrancy on buy/sell
Verify tokenInfo state consistency during re-entrant calls

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: If a token with transfer hooks is used, an attacker triggers a callback during router.buy() that re-enters Bonding.sell() on the same token. Sell reads stale tokenInfo (pre-buy), potentially getting incorrect price calculations.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Stale state manipulation; potential incorrect graduation parameters
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All holders of the affected token

threats.test_coverage_gap: No reentrancy test for buy/sell in Bonding

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring unusual call patterns

threats.title: Genesis.withdrawLeftAssetsAfterFinalized Can Drain Unclaimed Agent Tokens
threats.hypothesis_description: withdrawLeftAssetsAfterFinalized allows DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE (the FGenesis factory, controlled by ADMIN_ROLE) to withdraw ANY ERC20 token from the Genesis contract after finalization, including the agentTokenAddress. There is no check that the withdrawal amount preserves enough tokens to cover outstanding claimableAgentTokens. Admin can drain all unclaimed agent tokens before users claim them.
threats.investigation_rationale: The function has no logic to protect reserved tokens. The balance check only ensures the contract HAS the tokens, not that it's keeping enough for unclaimed users. This is a code-level defect since the function should exclude committed tokens.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No check against sum(claimableAgentTokens); allows withdrawing agentTokenAddress balance below committed claims

preconditions: Genesis finalized successfully with agent tokens distributed to claimableAgentTokens
DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE holder calls withdrawLeftAssetsAfterFinalized with token=agentTokenAddress
Users haven't claimed their tokens yet

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unrestricted token withdrawal including committed claim tokens
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: require(amount <= IERC20(token).balanceOf(address(this))) allows draining ALL tokens including those reserved for claims
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should check: for agentTokenAddress, amount <= balance - sum(claimableAgentTokens)

analysis_questions: Who controls DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE on Genesis (FGenesis factory)?
Who controls ADMIN_ROLE on FGenesis (can call withdrawLeftAssetsAfterFinalized)?
Is there any time delay or multi-sig requirement?
validation_requirements: Set up successful genesis, withdraw agentTokenAddress, verify user claims revert
Verify no protection against withdrawing committed tokens

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: After successful genesis, admin calls FGenesis.withdrawLeftAssetsAfterFinalized(genesisId, adminAddr, agentTokenAddress, totalBalance). All agent tokens are drained. Users calling claimAgentToken get reverts.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete loss of all unclaimed genesis agent tokens
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All genesis participants who haven't claimed
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Admin would need to return tokens manually

threats.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying withdrawal protects committed claim amounts

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Detectable by monitoring AssetsWithdrawn events for agentTokenAddress
